skill.name: gpt-image-edit
skill.description: Edit images with OpenAI GPT Image 2 (the `/edit` endpoint of ChatGPT Images 2.0) on RunComfy — bundled with the model's documented prompting patterns so the skill gets sharper output than naive prompting against the same model. Documents GPT Image Edit's strengths (preservation language, multilingual in-image text editing, multi-reference up to 10 images, layout / typography precision), the schema, and when to route to Nano Banana Edit / Flux Kontext / GPT Image 2 t2i instead. Calls `runcomfy run openai/gpt-image-2/edit` through the local RunComfy CLI. Triggers on "gpt image edit", "gpt-image-edit", "chatgpt image edit", "edit with gpt image 2", or any explicit ask to edit with this model.
skill.summary: Edit images using OpenAI GPT Image 2 with preservation language and multilingual text support
skill.author: runcomfy-com

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged 79 potential issues but manual review confirms all are false positives. The SKILL.md file is documentation, not executable code. Backtick patterns (59 detections) are markdown documentation of CLI syntax. Hardcoded URLs (11 detections) are legitimate RunComfy service endpoints. Path traversal flags (7 detections) document legitimate file paths. The "weak crypto" flags (2 detections) misidentified YAML syntax and section headers. The skill uses the RunComfy CLI for API calls, with documented security practices: tokens stored with 0600 permissions, no shell expansion of user input, and file size limits on downloads.

dangerous_patterns.title: Ruby/Shell Backtick Execution (False Positive)
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer detected 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' at 59 locations in SKILL.md. These are markdown documentation examples showing CLI command syntax (e.g., `runcomfy run openai/gpt-image-2/edit`). The file is documentation, not executable Ruby code. The backtick notation is standard markdown for inline code, not backtick command interpolation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: SKILL.md is explicitly markdown documentation; backticks in markdown produce code span elements, not shell execution. The skill is a Claude/Codex skill that documents CLI usage, not a code execution engine.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hardcoded URLs (False Positive)
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer flagged 11 hardcoded URLs as security concerns. These are legitimate RunComfy service documentation URLs and example placeholder URLs (e.g., `https://www.runcomfy.com`, `https://.../portrait.jpg`). No credential exfiltration or malicious destination detected.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: All URLs point to legitimate RunComfy service endpoints. The skill documents its own API usage, not exfiltrating data to unknown destinations.

dangerous_patterns.title: Path Traversal Sequences (False Positive)
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer flagged path traversal patterns at 7 locations. These are legitimate documentation of file paths used by the RunComfy CLI (e.g., `<absolute/path>` in CLI examples, `~/.config/runcomfy/token.json` for configuration storage). No actual path traversal attack detected.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The placeholders `<absolute/path>` are CLI argument documentation. `~/.config/runcomfy/token.json` documents legitimate configuration file location with proper security notes about 0600 permissions.

dangerous_patterns.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm (False Positive)
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer flagged 'weak cryptographic algorithm' at lines 4 and 160. Line 4 contains YAML front matter `description: >` (block scalar syntax). Line 160 is the 'Exit codes' section header. No cryptographic algorithms or weak crypto usage detected.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: YAML block scalar syntax and section headers do not constitute cryptographic algorithms. This is a clear misidentification by the static analyzer.

content.user_title: Edit Images with GPT Image 2 on RunComfy
content.value_statement: AI image editing requires precise prompts to preserve brand identity and layout. This skill bundles GPT Image 2 edit endpoint patterns so you get consistent preservation results without trial and error.
actual_capabilities: Edit images with preservation language to keep faces, poses, and brand marks unchanged
Rewrite multilingual text in images (Japanese kana, Cyrillic, Arabic, CJK, Latin scripts)
Use up to 10 reference images for multi-ref composition
Perform layout-precise edits (move headlines, swap CTAs, reposition elements)
Preserve identity across translated headline variants
Download generated images to specified output directory
limitations: Size must be one of: auto, 1024_1024, 1024_1536, or 1536_1024 — invalid values return 422 error
Maximum 10 images per request — batch editing requires multiple calls
Long compound prompts increase drift — split complex edits into multiple passes
Photorealism on portraits is weaker than Nano Banana Pro

use_cases.title: Multilingual Ad Localization
use_cases.description: Take a single source image asset and generate localized variants with the same headline in Japanese, Russian, Arabic, and other scripts. Preserve the original layout and brand identity.

use_cases.title: Brand-Safe Asset Updates
use_cases.description: Swap headlines or CTAs across a campaign while keeping all other brand elements intact. Use preservation language to ensure faces, logos, and color palette remain unchanged.

use_cases.title: Subject-Scene Composition
use_cases.description: Compose a subject from one photo into a different scene, using multi-reference support to preserve face, pose, and clothing from the original while matching lighting and color from the destination scene.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Background Swap
prompt_templates.prompt: Keep the person or product, pose, and face identity unchanged. Replace the background with [description of new background].
prompt_templates.scenario: When user wants to change the background while preserving subject

prompt_templates.title: Multilingual Text Rewrite
prompt_templates.prompt: Keep the photograph, layout, and brand mark exactly as in the input. Replace only the in-image headline. The new headline reads "[text]" in [script description], same position and font weight as before.
prompt_templates.scenario: When user wants to replace text in images for localization

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Reference Composition
prompt_templates.prompt: Compose subject from image 1 into the [scene description] from image 2. Match the [lighting/color description] of image 2. Keep subject identity (face, pose, clothing) from image 1 unchanged.
prompt_templates.scenario: When user wants to place a subject into a different scene

prompt_templates.title: Layout-Precise Repositioning
prompt_templates.prompt: Keep [elements to preserve] unchanged. Move the [element] from [original position] to [new position].
prompt_templates.scenario: When user wants to reposition specific elements within the image

output_examples.input: Edit the product photo to have a white studio background while keeping the bottle shape and label exactly the same
output_examples.output: The edited image shows the same product bottle with identical label and shape against a clean white studio background

output_examples.input: Replace the headline with '咖啡' in bold Chinese characters, keeping the rest of the poster layout and design unchanged
output_examples.output: The edited image contains the original poster layout with the new Chinese headline '咖啡' in bold at the same position

output_examples.input: Place the person from image 1 into the living room from image 2, matching the warm window lighting
output_examples.output: The edited image shows the person from the first image positioned in the living room scene with matching warm lighting

best_practices: Lead with preservation goals. Always state what should remain unchanged before describing the edit, as the model prioritizes early cues.
Quote multilingual text exactly. Use the exact characters for in-image text replacement rather than paraphrasing to ensure accurate rendering.
Use size:auto to preserve input ratio unless the edit explicitly changes framing. Override only when cropping or changing aspect ratio.
anti_patterns: Do not combine multiple complex edits in a single prompt. Each added scope increases drift. Split into multiple sequential passes instead.
Do not omit preservation goals. Without explicit instruction to preserve, the model may subtly modify faces, brands, or framing.
Do not paraphrase in-image text. Describe exact characters and script type rather than meaning to ensure accurate text rendering.

faq.question: What image formats does this skill support?
faq.answer: The skill accepts publicly-fetchable HTTPS image URLs. The RunComfy API handles the image processing internally.

faq.question: How many reference images can I use?
faq.answer: You can use up to 10 reference images. The first image is the primary input; remaining images serve as auxiliary cues for composition or style reference.

faq.question: Why does my edit change elements I wanted to keep?
faq.answer: Include explicit preservation language at the start of your prompt. State exactly which elements should remain unchanged, such as face, pose, brand mark, or layout.

faq.question: What size values are supported?
faq.answer: Size must be auto (preserve input ratio), 1024_1024 (1:1), 1024_1536 (2:3 portrait), or 1536_1024 (3:2 landscape). Other values return a 422 error.

faq.question: How do I use this skill in Claude Code or Codex?
faq.answer: Install the RunComfy CLI with npm i -g @runcomfy/cli, log in with runcomfy login, then invoke runcomfy run openai/gpt-image-2/edit with your prompt and image URLs.

faq.question: Is my image data sent to third parties?
faq.answer: Image and mask URLs you provide are fetched by the RunComfy model server, not your local machine. Generated files are downloaded directly to your specified output directory with a 2 GiB size cap.
